criteria.label: Define the curse of dimensionality and name its three canonical phenomena.
criteria.example: Write one sentence each for high-dimensional sparsity, distance concentration, and sample complexity (r^d grid cells).

criteria.label: Explain why fixed n makes local neighborhoods sparse as d grows.
criteria.example: Sketch: n=1000 points in [0,1]^d; as d rises, a radius-0.1 ball covers ≈0% of volume — fewer neighbors per query region.

criteria.label: Describe distance concentration and its effect on k-NN.
criteria.example: State that max/min distance ratio shrinks in high d, so k-th vs (k+1)-th neighbor gaps are tiny and votes become unstable.

criteria.label: State the r^d sample-complexity argument for grid coverage.
criteria.example: 10 bins per axis in d=6 → 10^6 cells; cite Bellman grid explosion as the same combinatorics.

criteria.label: Run a simulation showing distance concentration or k-NN decay with added noise dimensions.
criteria.example: NumPy script: print max/min distance ratio for d ∈ {2,10,50} or CV accuracy vs number of Gaussian noise columns appended.

criteria.label: Connect mitigations to linked topics without duplicating PCA or k-NN algorithms.
criteria.example: Bullet list: PCA lowers d; feature selection removes noise; trees/regularized linear avoid tiny neighborhoods — link topic IDs in prose.
